---
title: "Platelet WCM — TUI tinkering dashboard (design / envisaging)"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-06-15"
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    monofont: "Menlo"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
---

# TUI tinkering dashboard — design / envisaging

**Status: P0–P2 implemented (2026-06-16)** — the TUI lives in `wholecell/tui/`
(run `make tui`); P3 (rate overrides) not started. Build narrative:
`reports/lab-books/lab-book-2026-06-16-tui-bench.md`. The rest of this doc is
the original envisaging pass that scoped the work.

Envisaging pass on a terminal UI that
turns the platelet WCM into an interactive *experiment bench*: edit the
biology (concentrations, copy numbers, rate constants), knock out
receptors or whole pathways, hit **run**, watch the calcium trace stream
live, and compare against baseline — all from the terminal, sitting next
to the existing runscripts.

Scope decision (2026-06-15): build a **terminal TUI** (not an extension
of the Dash webapp), and **envisage all three depth phases** before
committing to how far the model refactor goes.

This doc has two halves, as the brief framed it:

1. **UI work** — what the TUI looks like and how it's built (§3–§5).
2. **How the model supports tinkering** — the three tiers of tunable
   values, and the refactor each tier needs (§6–§8). This is the harder
   and more interesting half.

---

## 1. Vision

A single full-screen terminal app — a "computational platelet bench" —
where one screen lets you:

- **Set up an experiment**: stimulus (agonist doses, extracellular Ca²⁺,
  timing), *genotype* (which receptors/enzymes are expressed and at what
  copy number), and *pharmacology* (which pathways are scaled or
  knocked out).
- **Run it** in the background while the UI stays responsive.
- **Watch** cytosolic / store Ca²⁺ (and IP₃, PKC, etc.) stream as the
  sim steps.
- **Compare** the result against the baseline or a previous run.
- **Save / reload** the whole setup as a reproducible config — because
  every knob already round-trips through `RunConfig.to_metadata()`.

The mental model for the user is *genotype + stimulus + pharmacology →
trace*, organised around the biology, not a flat form.

---

## 2. What already exists (we extend, not start cold)

- **`RunConfig`** (`reconstruction/platelet/run_config.py`) — a frozen
  per-sim knob bundle, threaded into the ODE as a *pure function*
  argument (`_ode_rhs(t, y, t_sim_start, config, step_inputs)`). The
  v0.62 refactor already killed in-place module-global mutation
  ("monkeypatching"). This is the clean foundation the whole idea rests
  on.
- **`run_platelet_sim(...)`** (`runscripts/manual/runPlateletSim.py`) —
  an importable function that takes a `RunConfig` (or its kwargs),
  builds `SimulationDataPlatelet`, runs the loop, and writes listeners.
  A TUI can call this directly in a worker thread.
- **Live CSV** — `RunConfig.live=True` already writes
  `simOut/live.csv` per step. Perfect for an in-terminal live trace.
- **`TableReader`** (`wholecell/io/tablereader.py`) — reads listener
  columns back for plotting / comparison.
- **The Dash webapp's job queue, plot tabs, and Docker plumbing** are
  *not* reused — a single-user local TUI doesn't need SQLite + a worker
  thread + `docker run`. (Though the webapp remains as-is; this is a
  parallel front-end, not a replacement.)

> **The TUI is intended to eventually replace the Dash webapp** (decision
> §12), not sit beside it forever. Textual can serve the same app over the
> web via `textual serve` / `textual-web`, which covers the webapp's
> remote-access use case; the Dash app stays only until the TUI reaches
> parity.

---

## 3. Why a TUI, and what to build it with

**Why a terminal UI fits this project.** It lives where the work already
happens (the runscripts, `PYTHONPATH=$PWD python …`), is keyboard-driven
and fast to iterate, needs no browser/server, and is trivially
scriptable. The cost is plots: full multi-panel matplotlib figures don't
render in a terminal, so live traces are ASCII/braille line charts and
the *publication* figures are handed off to the existing
`analysisPlatelet.py` (open externally). That split is fine — the TUI is
for *iterating*, the matplotlib pipeline is for *figures*.

**Recommended stack: [Textual](https://textual.textualize.io/)** (the
modern Python TUI framework, built on Rich).

| Need | Textual provides |
|------|------------------|
| Forms / editable fields | `Input`, `Switch`, `Select`, `Checkbox` |
| The big editable tables (rates, counts) | `DataTable` (sortable, editable cells) |
| Pathway grouping / collapsible sections | `Collapsible`, `TabbedContent`, `Tree` |
| Run without freezing the UI | `@work(thread=True)` workers + reactive state |
| Live trace | `textual-plotext` line charts (chosen, §12) |
| Progress | `ProgressBar` |
| Styling | TCSS (CSS-like), themeable |
| **Path to replace the webapp** (§12) | `textual serve` exposes the same app in a browser |

Alternatives considered: **Rich-only** (rendering, no interactivity — too
limited for editable forms), **prompt_toolkit** (lower-level, more
boilerplate), **urwid** (older, smaller widget set). Textual is the clear
pick and is actively maintained.

---

## 4. The core: three tiers of tunable values

This is the crux of "how the model supports tinkering." Every value a
user might want to edit lives in exactly one of three tiers — and **only
the first is per-run editable today**:

| Tier | Lives in | Per-run editable now? | Contents |
|------|----------|:---:|----------|
| **0 — Run knobs** | `RunConfig` | ✅ clean, pure-threaded | Extracellular Ca²⁺; 3 agonist doses + delay; feedback-loop gains (autocrine ADP/TXA₂); 6 curated scale/knockout knobs (COX-1/aspirin, integrin/Glanzmann, MCU, PMCA-k\_cat, P2Y1-desens, PLCβ-phos) |
| **1 — Rate constants** | `calcium-v0.6.toml` (~200 params) | ❌ loaded at **import time** → module globals | Every IP3R / SERCA / PMCA / SOCE / GPCR / PKC rate, K\_d, conductance, channel copy-number |
| **2 — Initial counts** | `species-v0.6.tsv` (78 species) | ❌ loaded at **import time** → `initial_counts` array | Resting copy number of every molecule / receptor / enzyme |
| **3 — Physics / structure** | hardcoded Python | ❌ — *and should stay that way* | Compartment volumes, Faraday/gas/T, subunits-per-channel |

Tier 0 maps essentially 1:1 onto form controls — it is almost free UI.
Tiers 1 and 2 are each loaded **once at module import** into module-level
globals (`_KINETICS`, `_MOLECULES`/`initial_counts`); making them per-run
is the real model work, and it is the *same architectural move* v0.62
already made for `RunConfig`: stop reading module globals, thread a
per-sim object through instead. Tier 3 should never be a UI knob (it's
physics and molecular architecture) — possibly excepting compartment
volumes, which are acknowledged estimates (cytosol ≈ 6 fL is a guess).

This tiering connects directly to the existing
[`kinetics-as-data-2026-05-22.qmd`](kinetics-as-data-2026-05-22.qmd)
plan, whose "four-level spectrum" describes the same axis from
Python-constants → fully-data-driven. Tier-1 editing is essentially that
doc's "Level 2" made interactive.

---

## 5. Knockouts decompose into two clean mechanisms

"Knock out a receptor or pathway" is not one operation — it's two, and
naming them clarifies the model work:

- **Expression knockout** = set a copy number to **0** (Tier 2). The
  natural way to kill a *receptor* (P2Y1, PAR1, PAR4, P2X1, TP, αIIbβ3)
  or an *enzyme* (SERCA, PMCA). Needs `count_overrides` + init-time
  count construction.
- **Activity knockout** = set a rate to **0** (Tier 1, or an existing
  Tier-0 scale). Six pathways already have this (aspirin → COX-1 = 0,
  MCU = 0, etc.). Generalising to *any* rate needs `rate_overrides`.

**Design judgement:** expression knockouts (Tier 2) are the
**higher-value, lower-risk** model investment. "Kill any
receptor/enzyme" is mostly achievable by zeroing copy numbers — one
clean mechanism — whereas the ~200-parameter rate-override machinery
(Tier 1) is really a *calibration / sensitivity-analysis* tool, not a
knockout tool. If forced to pick one of Phases 2 or 3, do Phase 2.

**The conservation gotcha.** Many species are pre-equilibrated
*sub-states* (SERCA E1/E2/E1Ca/E1PCa/E2PCa/E2P; the CaM ladder; STIM1
free/Ca-bound/dimer; P2Y1 active/inactive/desensitised). Zeroing one TSV
row in isolation breaks mass conservation and the resting steady state.
So a knockout must operate on the **logical entity** (sum of all its
conformational states), not a raw row. That implies a small **entity
map**:

```
"P2Y1"   -> {P2Y1_inactive[pl], P2Y1_active[pl], P2Y1_desensitised[pl]}
"SERCA"  -> {SERCA_E1[dts], SERCA_E2[dts], SERCA_E1Ca[dts], ...}
"αIIbβ3" -> {aIIbb3_resting[pl], aIIbb3_active[pl]}
...
```

curated once (design work, not hard), sitting above the flat species
list. The TUI's "knock out P2Y1" toggle then zeroes the whole set and
picks the right mechanism (count → 0) without the user knowing which
tier it touches.

---

## 6. Model refactor required, per tier

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: "Data flow. Tier-0 knobs already reach the ODE as pure args (solid). Tiers 1–2 (dashed) are loaded once at import into module globals today; the refactor moves their load to build-time so per-sim overrides can be merged in — the same 'no globals' move v0.62 made for RunConfig."
flowchart LR
  subgraph TUI
    K0[Tier 0 knobs]
    K1[Tier 1 rate overrides]
    K2[Tier 2 count overrides]
  end
  RC[RunConfig + overrides]
  K0 --> RC
  K1 -.-> RC
  K2 -.-> RC
  RC --> BUILD[build SimulationDataPlatelet]
  BUILD --> KIN[per-sim kinetics bundle]
  BUILD --> CNT[per-sim initial_counts]
  KIN --> ODE[_ode_rhs pure fn]
  CNT --> STATE[BulkMolecules]
  ODE --> RUN[sim loop]
  STATE --> RUN
  RUN --> LIS[listeners + live.csv]
  LIS --> TUI
```

**Tier 0 — none.** Already per-run and pure. The UI just has to *expose*
the full `RunConfig` (today the Configure tab surfaces only a handful of
its ~14 fields).

**Tier 2 — `count_overrides` (medium, well-contained).**

- Add `count_overrides: Mapping[str, int]` to `RunConfig` (default empty
  → byte-identical baseline).
- Move `initial_counts` array construction out of `internal_state.py`
  *import time* into sim-data build / `BulkMolecules` init, so overrides
  can be merged before the state vector is frozen.
- Add the entity map (§5) for logical-entity knockouts.
- Provenance: overrides already serialise via `to_metadata()`.

**Tier 1 — `rate_overrides` (most invasive, same pattern as v0.62).**

- Add `rate_overrides: Mapping[str, float]` to `RunConfig`, keyed by
  dotted TOML path (e.g. `"serca.cycle.k_cat"`).
- Move `_KINETICS = load_calcium_kinetics()` out of *module scope* into
  sim-data build, producing a **per-sim kinetics bundle** (frozen) with
  overrides merged onto the baseline TOML.
- Thread that bundle into `_ode_rhs(... , kinetics)` as an additional
  pure argument, replacing the ~30 module-level `K_*` / `N_*` / `GAMMA_*`
  reads. This is the bulk of the work — it touches the whole ODE — but
  it is mechanical and is the natural endpoint of the kinetics-as-data
  plan.

**Tier 3 — leave alone** (optionally expose volumes as an "advanced /
unvalidated" group, clearly flagged).

### 6.1 A correctness reason the refactor matters (not just UX)

A long-lived TUI process runs *many differently-tinkered sims back to
back*. Today, Tiers 1–2 are process-global (loaded at import). Mutating
them in-process to apply overrides would resurrect exactly the
module-global contamination v0.62 removed — run N's overrides would leak
into run N+1. So:

- **Phase 1 (RunConfig-only) runs are safe in-process today** — every
  knob is already pure-threaded.
- **Arbitrary Tier-1/2 overrides are only safe in-process *after* the
  build-time refactor.** Until then, the fallback is **subprocess per
  run** (fresh import each time) — simpler, but slower and it loses the
  easy in-process live-trace handoff.

This makes the refactor a *prerequisite* for the expert tinkering modes,
not merely a nicety — a useful argument when deciding how far to go.

**Decision (§12): subprocess per run.** A fresh process per run sidesteps
the contamination entirely (each run re-imports cleanly), so this refactor
is **off the P0+P1 critical path** and returns only if/when P2–P3 want
in-process multi-run. The cost is that the live trace comes from polling
the subprocess's `live.csv`, not an in-process callback.

---

## 7. The TUI itself — layout & interaction

A single dashboard screen, pathway-shaped, with a results pane. Sketch
(80×24 is the floor; it reflows wider):

```
┌─ Platelet WCM bench ──────────────────────── preset: [Agonist transient ▾] ┐
│ STIMULUS                          │ RESULTS                                 │
│  Thrombin   [ 1.0 ] nM            │  Ca²⁺_cyt (µM)   ▁▂▅█▇▅▃▂▁▁▁  peak 0.84 │
│  ADP        [10.0 ] µM            │  Ca²⁺_dts (µM)   ████▇▆▅▅▅▅▅  ───────── │
│  ATP_ex     [10.0 ] µM            │  IP₃ (nM)        ▁▃▆█▆▄▃▂▂▁▁  peak 412  │
│  Ca²⁺_ex    [ 1.2 ] mM            │  PKC active      ▁▁▂▄▆▇███▇▇             │
│  delay [0]s  length [200]s        │  t = 142 / 200 s   ████████░░  71%      │
│  □ run at rest                    │                                         │
├───────────────────────────────────┤  [ compare: baseline ▾ ]  overlay: ▢   │
│ RECEPTORS         expr   knockout │─────────────────────────────────────────│
│  P2Y1   [ 1500 ]   ●      ☐       │ MODIFIED FROM BASELINE (3)              │
│  PAR1   [ 1800 ]   ●      ☐       │  • thrombin_peak_nM  1.0 → 0.5          │
│  PAR4   [  900 ]   ●      ☐       │  • MCU scale         1.0 → 0.0  (KO)    │
│  P2X1   [ ... ]    ●      ☐       │  • PKC→PLCβ scale    1.0 → 0.3          │
│  TP     [ 1000 ]   ●      ☐       │                                         │
│  αIIbβ3 [80000 ]   ●      ☐       │  [ Run ]  [ Reset ]  [ Save preset ]    │
├───────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ PATHWAYS (scale 0–2, ✕ = knockout)   IP3R[1.0] SERCA[1.0] PMCA[1.0] SOCE[1.0]│
│   MCU[✕] NCX[1.0] | feedback: autocrine-ADP[on] autocrine-TXA₂[on] | aspirin□ │
│   ▸ Expert: rate table (~200)   ▸ Expert: count table (78)                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
 [Tab] move  [Enter] edit  [r] run  [c] compare  [s] save  [/]search  [?] help
```

Interaction principles:

- **Baseline-relative editing.** Every field shows its baseline default;
  editing flags it modified and the "MODIFIED FROM BASELINE" pane is the
  live diff. A run is always *"baseline + N changes,"* never a mystery
  blob.
- **Knockout as a first-class verb.** Per-receptor / per-pathway toggle
  that auto-selects the mechanism (count → 0 or scale → 0) via the
  entity map — the user thinks "kill P2Y1," not "which tier?".
- **Presets extend the existing four.** Add Aspirin, Glanzmann, and
  *user-saved* genotypes; presets are just named `RunConfig`s.
- **Expert tables behind a disclosure.** The ~200-rate and 78-count
  tables (Tiers 1/2) are searchable `DataTable`s, off by default, so the
  default screen stays biological and uncluttered.
- **Live trace + compare.** Poll the subprocess's `live.csv` into the
  results pane's plotext line charts as it runs; one keypress overlays the
  chosen baseline/previous run.

---

## 8. Backend wiring

```
TUI (Textual)
  └─ build RunConfig (+ overrides)        ← form state
  └─ @work(thread=True): subprocess → runPlateletSim.py <cfg> --live
        └─ writes simOut/…  +  simOut/live.csv
  └─ poll live.csv  → update plotext line charts + progress
  └─ on finish: TableReader → results pane; optional analysisPlatelet → external figure
```

- **A Textual worker thread supervises a subprocess** (decision §12) so
  the UI stays live; the trace comes from polling the subprocess's
  `live.csv`; final columns are read back with `TableReader`.
- **Subprocess per run** keeps each run's import-time globals isolated,
  so the Tier-1/2 build-time refactor (§6.1) is not on the P0+P1 path.
- **Full figures** are rendered by the figure-recipe registry (§9) and
  opened externally (`open` / image viewer) — the TUI iterates with the
  in-terminal sparklines; it does not render publication figures itself.

---

## 9. Figure generation on demand

Today, publication figures come from small bespoke scripts in
`runscripts/manual/` written per question and re-run by hand. We can do
better: a **registry of figure recipes** that the TUI (or the CLI) runs
on demand against a finished run. Half of this already exists.

### 9.1 Figures split into two kinds

| Kind | Input | Status today |
|------|-------|--------------|
| **Single-run** (e.g. `calcium_trace`, `scaffold_summary`) | one `simOut/` | ✅ registry exists; on-demand already works |
| **Multi-condition** (Phase 3 ±Ca²⁺; second-wave open/closed loop; perturbation knockouts) | **N runs** + labels/styles | ❌ no registry; bundled inside ad-hoc runners |

A single-run registry already lives in
`models/platelet/analysis/single/` — a base class
(`analysisPlot.py`, contract `do_plot(inputDir, plotOutDir, …)`), a
`TAGS` registry, importlib discovery, and the CLI
`analysisPlatelet.py <run_dir> --plot calcium_trace`, which writes
`plotOut/<name>.{pdf,svg,png}` (high-res PDF + SVG + 120-DPI PNG) via one
`exportFigure()` helper. **So "render figure X against a finished run, on
demand" is already solved for single-run figures.** A new one is
~40–60 lines (a couple of `TableReader` reads + matplotlib).

### 9.2 Why the bespoke scripts feel one-off

`runPhase3.py`, `runSecondWave.py`, `runPerturbation.py`,
`plotStoreLimitedFeedbacks.py`, `plotDownstreamModules.py`,
`plotIntegrin.py` are decoupled from that registry for one structural
reason: each *both* (a) runs several sims with different `RunConfig`s
*and* (b) plots them together. They're "one-off" because the experiment
(which conditions to run) is baked into the figure code. The single-run
registry can't host them because its contract is one input dir, not N.

### 9.3 The design — two moves, mostly refactoring

1. **Add a `MultiConditionAnalysisPlot` base class** taking
   `[(sim_out_dir, label, style), …]` → figure, with a `comparison/`
   registry beside `single/`. Refactor the *plotting half* of each
   bespoke script into one of these (≈ same line count, minus the
   sim-running boilerplate).
2. **Separate the experiment recipe from the figure recipe.** The
   bespoke scripts' "run these N conditions" half is *exactly what the
   TUI already does interactively* — a base `RunConfig` plus named
   variants (aspirin = `cox1_factor 0`, Glanzmann = `integrin_act_scale
   0`, EDTA = `ca_ex_mM 0`). So a comparison figure becomes: *"run my
   current config + these variants, overlay with recipe Z."* The figure
   recipe consumes the resulting output dirs and never knows how they
   were produced.

In the TUI: after any run, list the applicable recipes (single-run ones
always; comparison ones once a variant set has been run), render on a
keypress → `plotOut/<name>.{pdf,svg,png}` → open externally. The
"compare runs" feature and the "render figure" feature become the **same
machinery**.

### 9.4 Payoffs beyond the TUI

- **Fills a real gap.** There is currently *no canonical figure-set
  regeneration script* — release figures (`reports/figures/v0.5/`,
  `v0.61/`) are re-run by hand and committed. A recipe registry *is* the
  regeneration pipeline: "regenerate the v0.7 figures" = run the
  registry. Worth it independently of the TUI.
- **DRY + consistent style.** Stops re-deriving `TableReader` reads and
  thesis styling in every script; a shared rcParams/theme on top of the
  existing `exportFigure` PDF+SVG+PNG split gives uniform thesis-grade
  output.

### 9.5 Caveats (the actual labour)

- Untangling experiment-from-figure in each bespoke script is the work —
  every one needs splitting into a `RunConfig`-set recipe + a figure
  recipe.
- Some scripts also emit `.npz` / `.json` data sidecars for
  reproducibility; the recipe pattern must preserve those.
- Matplotlib render time means this is a *render-to-file-and-open*
  action, not a live in-terminal redraw (the sparklines cover live).

---

## 10. Reproducibility & guardrails

- **Every run is reproducible by construction.** `RunConfig` +
  `to_metadata()` already capture the complete knob set; a tinkered run
  is self-documenting and shareable as a JSON preset. Extending it with
  `count_overrides` / `rate_overrides` keeps that property.
- **Goldens are untouchable.** Dolan 5/5 and the golden tests always run
  the *baseline* `RunConfig`; tinkering cannot regress them, because
  overrides default to empty → byte-identical.
- **Stability.** Arbitrary counts/rates can make the BDF solver stiff /
  non-convergent or produce negative counts. The TUI should: show
  baseline + units + a plausible range per field; catch sim failure
  (the run function raises → show the error in the diff pane, don't
  crash the UI); and label the expert tables as "here be dragons."
- **Validation of edits.** Soft range hints, not hard locks (research
  tool — the user may *want* an extreme value), but flag
  out-of-physiological-band values, consistent with the existing
  caution that volume/flux estimates cross biological bands.

---

## 11. Phasing

| Phase | Scope | Model change | Effort | Payoff |
|-------|-------|--------------|:---:|--------|
| **P0** | Textual skeleton: stimulus + run + live trace + results, in-process, Tier-0 only | none | small | proves the loop end-to-end |
| **P1** | Full `RunConfig` surfaced, grouped by subsystem; curated knockouts; compare-to-baseline; save/load presets | none | small–med | ~80% of the "tinkering" feel |
| **P2** | `count_overrides` + init-time counts + entity map → knock out / titrate *any* receptor/enzyme; count table | medium, contained | medium | the real knockout story |
| **P3** | `rate_overrides` + build-time kinetics bundle threaded into the ODE → edit *any* rate; rate table | invasive (whole ODE), same pattern as v0.62 | large | expert / sensitivity mode |

P0+P1 deliver a genuinely useful bench (and a strong demo / thesis
artifact) with **zero model risk**, because Tier 0 is already clean. P2
is the highest-value model investment (knockouts). P3 is real but should
be gated on whether "edit any rate" is an actual research need vs. a
nice-to-have — it's the kinetics-as-data refactor in disguise.

**Figure recipes (§9) are an orthogonal workstream**, not a phase gate.
Single-run recipes already work, so the TUI gets them in P1 for free
(just expose `analysisPlatelet.py`). The `MultiConditionAnalysisPlot`
base class + refactoring the bespoke comparison scripts is independent of
the tier refactor and can land whenever — its biggest standalone payoff
(a canonical figure-regeneration pipeline) is worth doing regardless of
how far the TUI itself goes.

---

## 12. Decisions (resolved 2026-06-15)

1. **Scope: P0 + P1.** Build the Textual skeleton and surface the full
   `RunConfig` + curated knockouts + compare-to-baseline + save/load
   presets. **Defer P2 (count overrides) and P3 (rate overrides)** until
   there's a concrete need — so the build touches Tier 0 only and carries
   zero model risk.
2. **Run worker: subprocess per run** (not critical, can revisit). A
   fresh process per run means import-time globals can't leak between
   runs, so **the Tier-1/2 build-time refactor (§6.1) is off the critical
   path** — consistent with the P0+P1-only scope. Trade-off accepted:
   live trace comes from the `live.csv` the subprocess writes (poll the
   file), not an in-process callback.
3. **Entity map lives in `reconstruction/platelet/`**, alongside the
   species loader. (Relevant only when P2 lands, but fixing the home
   now.)
4. **Live trace: line charts** via `textual-plotext` (one dependency),
   not Rich sparklines. The §7 mockup's bar glyphs are illustrative; the
   real results pane is plotext line charts.
5. **The TUI will eventually replace the Dash webapp** — it is the
   intended primary front-end, not a parallel toy. Plan for `textual
   serve` to cover the webapp's remote-access use case; the Dash app
   stays only until the TUI reaches parity.
6. **Versioned params: yes** — every saved preset records the param
   version it was authored against (`calcium-v0.6` / `species-v0.6`) so
   overrides don't silently drift on a v0.7 bump.
7. **Figure recipes (§9): the final piece — defer.** Build the
   `MultiConditionAnalysisPlot` base + refactor the bespoke comparison
   scripts *after* the UI itself is done. Single-run recipes already work
   and come for free in P1; the multi-condition registry (and its
   regeneration-pipeline payoff) waits until last.
```
